story:
We needed to make a great first impression on the Shaman. I tried to focus, but I kicked a rock off the path, and a priest silently scolded me by putting it back and glaring—holy disapproval at its finest. Alora told me to stop kicking rocks and reminded me the priests weren’t thrilled we’d brought the Elite Guard to their gates. The one bit of comfort: the Guard wouldn’t cross into holy ground. Still, it’s hard to sleep with an arrest squad camping outside.

We decided our best route was impressing the Shaman. Alora remembered a priest who had actually laughed at one of my jokes—a miracle around here. We found him in the kitchen. His name wasn’t Flin or Fron; it was Grinfo, and I bungled the greeting, offering a handshake on a world where they bow and point. I turned on the charm anyway. We needed help avoiding the Elite Guard, and the Shaman was our only shot.

Grinfo offered the strangest tip: the Shaman’s current name was supposedly unpronounceable, so unpronounceable not even the Shaman could say it. He’d changed it from a supposedly easy one, Joequellfeellian. The new name was carved above the temple door. Maybe, since we came from far away, we’d recognize the symbols.

Alora reminded me—again—that she’s the linguist, the android who knows thousands of languages and built my translator. I reminded her—again—that I took one course and am still the captain. We arrived beneath the arch and stared. Three letters, plain as sunrise: B, O, B.

Grinfo asked if we could say it. I told him I could. He warned me that if I was lying, the Shaman would toss me to the Elite Guard. Noted.

That night we were invited to dine with the Shaman, a short man in a burnt orange robe and hat, with a black-and-white braided beard and vine necklaces—more forest than temple. After a prayer and the chant, “The way provided,” he asked Alora if she had incapacitated some of Targon’s men. She nodded and explained how Freena had hidden us and suffered for it, and how she couldn’t let the guards hurt her. The Shaman smiled—pompous windbags, he called them—and admitted he suspected Targon hadn’t told him everything. Alora and I traded a glance. We weren’t about to confess we weren’t from this world.

Grinfo led me to the podium. He announced I would speak the Shaman’s name. The hall buzzed with nerves and skepticism. I took a breath and said it. Bob.

Several priests toppled off their seats. The rest oohed and ahhed and tried to imitate me. They failed. The Shaman bowed his head to me. He’d only heard his name once before, and I’d said it perfectly. He offered us protection and promised to smooth things over with the Elite Guard. I must have nailed that first impression.

Then he asked Grinfo for a drink and waited until we were alone. He lowered his voice. He knew what we were. Only a god could say his name, he said, because a god had given it to him. He smiled as if we shared a pleasant secret.

That’s when the twist landed: around here, pronouncing “Bob” isn’t just a parlor trick—it’s divine.
